pattern: A qualifying window is exactly a maximal run of one repeated character whose length equals k, so grouping the string into runs answers the question directly.
hints: Break the string into maximal runs of a single repeated character; a run's boundaries automatically differ from whatever character comes right before or after it.

A window of length k made of one character only satisfies the 'different neighbor' rules when it lines up exactly with one of these maximal runs -- a shorter slice sitting inside a longer run still has the same character on both sides of it.
So the answer is simply: does any maximal run have length exactly k?

edge_inputs.description: statement example, run of exactly k in the middle
statement example, no run of the right length
k equals the whole string length, single run
k = 1 on a string with no two adjacent equal characters
a run longer than k should NOT count -- a shorter slice inside it still touches same-character neighbors
run sits at the very start of the string, no left neighbor to check
run sits at the very end of the string, no right neighbor to check

stress.description: random lowercase string with random k
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(100, size))
    s = ''.join(rng.choice('ab') for _ in range(n))
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [s, k]
